title: Sonnet 30 (Fire And Ice) by Edmund Spenser — Love Poetry Lines
content:
My love is like to ice, and I to fire:
how comes it then that this her cold so great
is not dissolv'd through my so hot desire,
but harder grows, the more I her entreat?

Or how comes it that my exceeding heat
is not delayed by her heart frozen cold,
but that I burn much more in boiling sweat,
meaning: "Sonnet 30 (Fire And Ice)" by Edmund Spenser is a love poem consisting of 17 lines. The poet explores themes of love through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "My love is like to ice, and I to fire: how comes it then that this her cold so great...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Edmund Spenser's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
